question: What is the key difference between a static VLAN and a dynamic VLAN?
choice_a: Static VLANs use 802.1Q tagging while dynamic VLANs use ISL tagging
choice_b: Static VLANs require a DHCP server while dynamic VLANs use manual IP assignment
choice_c: Static VLANs are permanent while dynamic VLANs reset on every reboot
choice_d: Static VLANs assign ports manually while dynamic VLANs assign membership based on MAC address or username
explanation: Static VLANs are the most common and predictable — an admin manually assigns each port. Dynamic VLANs assign VLAN membership automatically based on MAC address or user credentials preserving membership when devices move.
aspect_key: static manual assignment vs dynamic mac/user-based assignment
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: How does IEEE 754 store non-integer floating-point values in 32-bit format?
choice_a: Using two separate 16-bit integers where one holds the whole part and one holds the decimal part
choice_b: Using a 32-bit integer with an implicit decimal point always fixed at bit 16
choice_c: By storing values as ASCII text codes that the CPU converts on each calculation
choice_d: With 1 sign bit; 8 exponent bits; and 23 significand bits structured like scientific notation
explanation: IEEE 754 32-bit float: 1 bit for sign; 8 bits for exponent; 23 bits for significand. This is like scientific notation (e.g. 625.9 = 0.6259 x 10^3). The floating decimal point can represent a wide range of magnitudes.
aspect_key: ieee 754 uses sign exponent and significand to store floats
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 13 - representing numbers and letters with binary/Transcript.md

question: After saving a running configuration to a TFTP server how can the administrator verify the backup is complete and accurate?
choice_a: By running show startup-config on the switch which automatically synchronizes with the TFTP server
choice_b: By pinging the TFTP server from the switch CLI which confirms the transfer was successful
choice_c: By checking for the filename in the TFTP server application and opening the saved file in a text editor to inspect the configuration
choice_d: By running copy tftp running-config which will succeed only if the original transfer was error-free
explanation: The SolarWinds TFTP Server GUI shows received files. Opening the saved .cfg file in Notepad++ or a similar text editor lets the administrator inspect interface settings; IP addresses; NAT rules; and ACL entries confirming the backup content is correct.
aspect_key: verify tftp backup by checking file in server and opening in text editor
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 15 - SolarWinds TFTP Server/Transcript.md

question: What problem did Unicode solve that ASCII could not?
choice_a: Unicode replaced binary with hexadecimal for more efficient data storage
choice_b: Unicode added encryption to text data preventing eavesdropping on shared networks
choice_c: ASCII values 128-255 were used differently by different systems causing garbled text; Unicode provides a single universal standard for all languages and symbols
choice_d: Unicode replaced ASCII by reducing character encoding from 7 bits to 4 bits
explanation: Extended ASCII (values 128-255) was defined differently by different countries and vendors. Files crossing incompatible systems displayed garbled characters called mojibake. Unicode (introduced 1992) resolves this with a single universal encoding covering all world languages; symbols; and emoji.
aspect_key: unicode resolves extended ascii incompatibility across systems
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 13 - representing numbers and letters with binary/Transcript.md

question: Why is the enable password command considered weaker than enable secret?
choice_a: Enable password requires a RADIUS server for authentication while enable secret uses local authentication
choice_b: Enable password only applies to console access while enable secret applies to Telnet and SSH as well
choice_c: Enable password is stored in the running-config as a plaintext or weakly encoded value while enable secret stores an MD5 hash
choice_d: Enable password limits access to user EXEC mode only while enable secret applies to both user and privileged EXEC
explanation: enable password uses a simple reversible cipher (type 7 encoding) that is easily decoded. enable secret uses MD5 hashing which is computationally difficult to reverse. Anyone with access to the running-config or startup-config can decode an enable password in seconds.
aspect_key: enable password uses weak encoding while enable secret uses md5 hash
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: What decimal value does uppercase A have in ASCII?
explanation: In the ASCII encoding standard uppercase A is assigned decimal value 65. Lowercase a is 97. These values are standardized so any system using ASCII will interpret byte value 65 as the letter A.
aspect_key: uppercase A equals decimal 65 in ascii
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 12 - What Is Binary/Transcript.md

question: What binary value does the ASCII character lowercase 'a' have given its decimal value of 97?
explanation: Decimal 97 in binary: 64 + 32 + 1 = 97; place values active are 64 (2^6) and 32 (2^5) and 1 (2^0). That gives 01100001. Compare to uppercase A = 65 = 01000001; only bit 5 differs.
aspect_key: lowercase a is decimal 97 equal to binary 01100001
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 13 - representing numbers and letters with binary/Transcript.md

question: Why does a 32-bit operating system have an addressable RAM limit of approximately 4 GB?
choice_a: The OS kernel uses half of RAM for system processes leaving only half for user programs
choice_b: 32-bit systems use hexadecimal addressing which caps out at 4 GB by definition
choice_c: 4 GB is a hardware enforced limit set by BIOS regardless of OS bit-width
choice_d: A 32-bit address bus can reference at most 2^32 unique byte addresses which equals approximately 4 GB of memory
explanation: Memory addresses in a 32-bit system are 32-bit values. 2^32 = 4294967296 unique addresses. Since each address typically refers to one byte the maximum addressable RAM is ~4 GB. 64-bit systems extend this to terabytes.
aspect_key: 32-bit address space limits addressable ram to approximately 4 gb
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 13 - representing numbers and letters with binary/Transcript.md

question: What is mojibake and what causes it?
choice_a: A Japanese encoding format used as a replacement for ASCII in East Asian systems
choice_b: Garbled text that occurs when a file encoded in one character set is read by a system using a different incompatible character set
choice_c: A Unicode error that occurs when emoji characters exceed the 3-byte UTF-8 encoding limit
choice_d: A type of binary overflow that happens when an ASCII value exceeds decimal 127
explanation: Mojibake is the garbled or nonsensical characters produced when text is interpreted using the wrong character encoding. It commonly occurred when files created on systems using one extended-ASCII variant were opened on systems with a different variant for values 128-255.
aspect_key: mojibake is garbled text from incompatible character encoding
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 13 - representing numbers and letters with binary/Transcript.md

question: What does PoE resistive discovery prevent?
choice_a: It prevents unauthorized devices from connecting to the switch by rejecting unknown MAC addresses
choice_b: It prevents switch overheating by limiting the number of simultaneous PoE connections
choice_c: It prevents accidental power delivery to non-PoE devices by checking for a ~25 kOhm signature before applying full power
choice_d: It prevents the PSE from supplying more than 100W to any single port regardless of the PoE standard in use
explanation: Before applying full PoE power a PSE applies a low test voltage and checks for a ~25 kOhm resistive signature on the cable. Only a valid PD has this signature. Non-PoE devices do not and are protected from receiving full power.
aspect_key: resistive discovery checks 25 kohm signature before applying power
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: How does a switch learn which port a device is connected to?
choice_a: It queries a central DHCP server that maps MAC addresses to switch ports
choice_b: It reads the source MAC address on incoming frames and associates it with the port the frame arrived on
choice_c: It broadcasts a discovery packet and waits for devices to reply with their port numbers
choice_d: It uses ARP to resolve IP addresses to MAC addresses and stores the result in its routing table
explanation: The learning process: when a frame arrives the switch reads the source MAC address and records it along with the ingress port in the MAC address table. Future frames destined for that MAC are forwarded directly to the learned port.
aspect_key: switch learns source mac and ingress port from each incoming frame
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches
user_feedback: "incoming frames" needs to say "frames from the computer" because the word incoming doesn't have a clear noun in that sentence.

question: A phone is plugged into a switch access port that is also connected to a PC. The port is configured with both an access VLAN for data and a voice VLAN. How does the switch differentiate the traffic?
choice_a: The switch inspects TCP port numbers to separate voice (UDP/RTP) traffic from data (TCP) traffic at Layer 4
choice_b: The switch uses the IP DSCP marking to assign traffic to the data or voice VLAN
choice_c: The IP phone tags its own voice traffic with the voice VLAN ID while the PC sends untagged data traffic; the switch separates them by presence or absence of the 802.1Q tag and its VLAN ID
choice_d: The switch differentiates traffic by source MAC address since IP phones have a different OUI than PCs
explanation: Access port with voice VLAN: the IP phone understands 802.1Q and tags its voice frames with the voice VLAN ID. The PC sends untagged traffic. The switch receives both: tagged frames go to the voice VLAN; untagged frames stay in the access (data) VLAN. This allows one physical port to support both simultaneously.
aspect_key: ip phone tags voice traffic; pc sends untagged; switch separates by tag presence
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 17 - Trunking and 802.1Q/Transcript.md

question: How does a bridge perform media conversion and what modern network role does it fill?
choice_a: Bridges convert between IPv4 and IPv6 address formats to connect legacy and modern subnets
choice_b: Bridges regenerate electrical signals between two cable segments; modern switches still use this for PoE delivery
choice_c: Bridges can connect different media types (copper on one side; fiber on the other) forwarding frames by MAC; modern switches are essentially multiport bridges fulfilling this same function
choice_d: Bridges translate between 802.1Q and ISL encapsulation; modern switches still perform this translation for legacy devices
explanation: A bridge connects different network media types (e.g. copper to fiber) and makes forwarding decisions based on MAC addresses. Modern multiport switches are the direct evolution of bridges — they do the same MAC-based forwarding with many more ports and higher speed.
aspect_key: bridges connect different media by mac forwarding; switches are multiport bridges
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What is the MAC address table also called?
choice_a: The IP routing table
choice_b: The ARP cache
choice_c: The forwarding database
choice_d: The VLAN assignment table
explanation: The MAC address table — which maps learned MAC addresses to switch ports — is also called the forwarding database. Both terms refer to the same data structure the switch uses to make frame forwarding decisions.
aspect_key: mac address table is also called the forwarding database

question: What does NAT track to ensure internet replies reach the correct internal host?
choice_a: NAT tracks the VLAN ID of the original request to route replies to the correct segment
choice_b: NAT tracks the destination IP address of the server to forward replies to the correct subnet
choice_c: NAT tracks which internal host originated each session so it can map the incoming reply back to the correct private IP
choice_d: NAT tracks ARP entries and uses them to forward reply traffic to the requesting host
explanation: When an internal host sends traffic through NAT the router records the session mapping (internal IP and port; external IP and port; and translated public IP and port). When the reply arrives the router looks up this session record to restore the original private destination IP.
aspect_key: nat tracks original session to map internet replies back to the correct private host
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What is the operational difference between Cisco's term trunking and other vendors' term tagging?
choice_a: Trunking refers to LACP link aggregation on Cisco equipment; tagging refers to 802.1Q VLAN marking on non-Cisco equipment
choice_b: Trunking means carrying multiple VLANs over one link; tagging describes the 802.1Q marking mechanism — different terms for the same concept depending on vendor
choice_c: Trunking is a Layer 2 concept while tagging is Layer 3; they operate at different OSI layers
choice_d: Cisco uses trunking for ISL and tagging for 802.1Q; the two terms describe different encapsulation protocols
explanation: Cisco commonly calls the feature trunk and the port a trunk port. Other vendors (including IEEE documentation) describe the mechanism as tagging — adding a VLAN tag to frames. Both terms describe the same behavior: one physical link carrying traffic for multiple VLANs using frame marking.
aspect_key: trunking (cisco) and tagging (other vendors) describe the same multi-vlan link feature
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 17 - Trunking and 802.1Q/Transcript.md
